Title: Ethylene promotes SMAX1 accumulation to inhibit arbuscular mycorrhiza symbiosis

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: SMXL family transcriptional repressor; proteolytic target of KAI2-SCFMAX2 signaling
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SMAX1 is required for ethylene-mediated suppression of arbuscular mycorrhiza (AM) colonization in Lotus japonicus. 2. smax1 mutants show increased AM colonization and are resistant to ACC-induced inhibition of colonization. 3. Ethylene signaling via EIN2 promotes nuclear accumulation of SMAX1 protein without altering SMAX1 transcript abundance. 4. SMAX1 and ethylene suppress an overlapping set of AM-relevant genes including strigolactone biosynthesis genes and common symbiosis genes.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutants (smax1-2, smax1-3); ACC treatment; genetic interaction with ein2a ein2b; RNA-seq; confocal imaging of SMAX1D2-GFP; Western blot; KAR1 rescue assay; AM colonization phenotyping
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Transcript not changed by ethephon or ein2a ein2b in non-colonized roots; protein accumulates in root nuclei after ACC treatment
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low/sparse nuclear protein signal in untreated roots; strong nuclear accumulation after ACC in wild type but not in ein2a ein2b
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Reducing SMAX1 activity could enhance AM establishment and mineral nutrient acquisition under low-input conditions; deployment should consider interaction with ethylene and karrikin/KL signaling and possible pleiotropic developmental effects
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Mineral nutrients (especially phosphate; also nitrogen via AM symbiosis)
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Root-associated nutrient uptake via arbuscular mycorrhizal interface
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Improves plant access to poorly available mineral nutrients, especially phosphate, through AM symbiosis
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes inhibition of AM development and thereby reduces nutrient-acquiring symbiosis when accumulated; loss of SMAX1 enhances colonization
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely pleiotropic effects through broader KAI2/KL signaling and ethylene homeostasis; exact growth tradeoffs not established in this paper
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ACC; ethephon; KAR1; AM fungal inoculation; low-phosphate growth conditions
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: pUbiquitin for SMAX1D2-GFP; endogenous SMAX1 promoter not sufficient for detectable signal in Lotus roots
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SMAX1 is categorized here as Common Gene because the paper describes it as a transcriptional repressor/signaling hub rather than a canonical TF. Ethylene-promoted accumulation is KAI2-independent, whereas KAR1 counteracts accumulation in a KAI2-dependent manner. Arabidopsis and Nicotiana assays suggest the mechanism is conserved beyond AM hosts.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Carlactone pathway intermediate(s) supporting strigolactone production
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively cleaves a carotenoid precursor in the strigolactone biosynthetic pathway to generate apocarotenoid intermediates leading to strigolactones
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point not explicitly established; expression is a major regulated node in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CCD7 transcript is repressed by ethephon/ACC treatment before colonization. 2. CCD7 expression is elevated in smax1 mutants, overlapping with ethylene-responsive regulation. 3. Reduced strigolactone biosynthesis inferred from repression of CCD7 contributes to reduced AM colonization, because low-dose GR24^5DS rescues ACC inhibition.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; pharmacological rescue with GR24^5DS of ACC-treated roots; overlap analysis with smax1 transcriptome
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced in AM roots and suppressed by ethylene treatment; increased in smax1 roots
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher in AM-associated conditions and ethylene-insensitive backgrounds relative to ethylene-treated wild type
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining CCD7 expression could support strigolactone-mediated fungal activation and improve AM-assisted phosphate acquisition, especially under ethylene-promoting stress
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stimulates AM fungal spore germination and hyphal branching, promoting symbiosis and nutrient acquisition
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes AM colonization by supporting strigolactone biosynthesis; ethylene-mediated repression reduces colonization
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strigolactones also affect plant architecture and parasitic weed interactions, though not tested here
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ACC; ethephon; GR24^5DS; AM fungal inoculation; low phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core status based on repeated emphasis and functional rescue of the strigolactone branch, though direct CCD7 transgenics were not used in this paper

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Processes CCD7-derived apocarotenoid intermediate to produce carlactone in the strigolactone biosynthetic pathway
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CCD8 is repressed by ethylene treatment in non-colonized roots. 2. CCD8 expression is elevated in smax1 mutants, showing overlap between SMAX1 and ethylene regulation. 3. Repression of CCD8 likely contributes to lower strigolactone output and reduced AM establishment.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; comparative transcriptomics with smax1 and ethephon-treated roots; GR24^5DS rescue of ACC inhibition supports pathway role
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: AM-induced and ethylene-repressed; increased in smax1 mutants
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Enhanced in colonization-permissive conditions relative to ethylene-treated roots
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target to sustain strigolactone production for AM recruitment under stress or high ethylene conditions
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Activates AM fungi for root colonization and supports nutrient acquisition
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports AM by promoting strigolactone biosynthesis; repression associates with ethylene-induced AM inhibition
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible architecture or rhizosphere signaling effects not analyzed here
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ACC; ethephon; low phosphate; AM fungal inoculation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included among the strongest overlapping SMAX1/ethylene targets with known AM function

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: EIN2 family membrane-associated ethylene signaling protein; CEND nuclear-acting domain
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Signal transduction activation leading to nuclear ethylene responses and promotion of SMAX1 accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Regulation of AM-associated nutrient-acquisition symbiosis via transcriptional suppression of strigolactone biosynthesis and common symbiosis genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes repression of AM-relevant genes indirectly via ethylene signaling; promotes SMAX1 protein accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential ethylene signaling component required for ethylene-mediated suppression of AM and associated mineral nutrient acquisition
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Ethylene derived from ACC or ethephon
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Acts in ethylene signaling pathway downstream of receptors; specific complex not resolved here
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Part of ethylene-SMAX1 feedback circuit proposed in the paper
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Loss of EIN2B increases AM colonization, and ein2a ein2b roots are largely insensitive to ACC/ethephon suppression of colonization. 2. Epidermis-targeted expression of constitutively active EIN2B-CEND restores suppression of AM, indicating epidermal action. 3. EIN2 is required for ethylene-induced SMAX1 accumulation in nuclei.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (ein2b and ein2a ein2b); ACC/ethephon treatments; tissue-specific complementation with EIN2B-CEND; confocal SMAX1D2-GFP quantification
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Functionally active in epidermis for AM suppression; constitutively active CEND suppresses colonization when expressed ubiquitously or epidermally
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Reducing epidermal ethylene signaling could preserve AM-mediated nutrient uptake under stress, but may compromise normal ethylene responses
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Mineral nutrients (especially phosphate) obtained via AM symbiosis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Suppresses AM colonization and thus limits mycorrhiza-assisted nutrient acquisition
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Ethylene signaling has broad stress and developmental roles; weakening EIN2 may impair other ethylene-dependent responses
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: ER membrane; CEND translocates to nucleus
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ACC; ethephon; tissue-specific promoter-driven expression; AM fungal inoculation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: EIN2B is the major paralog affecting AM in Lotus, whereas EIN2A had little or no effect in this study
